planting.note: Plant outdoors only after all danger of frost has passed and temperatures remain consistently warm. In frost-free climates (zones 10–11), can be planted year-round in a sheltered, shaded spot.

directions: Propagate from stem cuttings taken in spring or summer, rooting them in moist perlite or a well-draining potting mix in a warm, humid environment. Plant in deep to partial shade with rich, consistently moist but well-draining soil amended with compost. Water regularly to keep the root zone evenly moist, and mist foliage occasionally to raise humidity in dry conditions. Bring indoors before the first frost; it makes an excellent houseplant in a bright, indirect-light position.

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before the last expected frost date. Surface-sow on moist seed-starting mix and keep under bright indirect light; do not cover seeds as light aids germination.

directions: Sow seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost date, pressing them lightly onto the surface of moist seed-starting mix and providing bright indirect light; germination may be slow and erratic. Transplant seedlings into partial shade once nighttime temperatures stay reliably above 50°F, spacing them 8–12 inches apart in rich, humus-heavy soil that drains well. Keep soil consistently moist and avoid allowing plants to dry out, as this species favors the cool, humid conditions of its Himalayan origin. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering and protect plants from drying winds, which can stress foliage rapidly.

planting.note: Direct-sow outdoors on or just after the last frost date once the soil has warmed. Press seeds onto moist soil surface in a shaded spot; do not bury them, as light supports germination.

directions: Direct-sow seeds on the soil surface in a moist, partially shaded bed after the last frost date, pressing them in gently without covering since light improves germination rates. Thin seedlings to 8–12 inches apart once they reach 2–3 inches tall, choosing the strongest plants and composting the rest. Water consistently throughout the growing season to keep soil evenly moist, especially during dry spells. Allow a few plants to set seed at the end of the season so the colony naturalizes; self-sown seedlings often emerge reliably in subsequent years.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once the soil is workable and hard frosts are unlikely. In colder parts of its range (zones 7–8), choose a sheltered, south-facing wall or overhang to protect the woody base from freezing.

directions: Take softwood cuttings in late spring or early summer and root them in a moist, gritty mix in a shaded, humid environment; pot on once roots are established. Plant in a sheltered, partially shaded position in fertile, moisture-retentive but well-draining soil, and mulch the root zone deeply in autumn to protect the woody crown from winter cold in zones 7–8. Cut back top growth after frost kills it and allow the plant to re-sprout from the base in spring. Divide established clumps every few years in spring to maintain vigor and to propagate additional plants.

planting.note: Place outdoors only after temperatures remain above 60°F at night. In frost-prone climates, grow in containers that can be brought indoors for the winter.

directions: Propagate by leaf-section cuttings or rhizome divisions placed in a warm, humid propagator on moist perlite or seed-starting mix; avoid burying the cutting deeply. Grow in bright to moderate indirect shade in well-draining, humus-rich potting mix with good air circulation to prevent powdery mildew. Water at the base rather than overhead, allowing the top inch of soil to dry between waterings to discourage root rot. Maintain temperatures above 60°F year-round and bring container plants indoors well before autumn temperatures drop.

planting.note: Plant outdoors after the last frost once nighttime lows stay reliably above 60°F. Provide a spacious container or garden bed to accommodate the exceptionally large spread of mature foliage.

directions: Propagate from rhizome sections or stem cuttings set in moist, humus-rich mix in a warm location; the thick rhizome should sit at or just above the soil surface. Plant in deep shade or bright filtered light in very rich, moisture-retentive soil and water generously and consistently, as this species is intolerant of drought due to its large leaf surface area. Provide a large container or open garden bed to accommodate the impressive spread, and feed monthly with a balanced fertilizer to sustain vigorous leaf production. Move container plants indoors before the first frost and keep them warm and well-watered through winter.
